statement.markdown: Given a doubly linked list, delete the head node and return the modified list. If the list is empty, return an empty list. If the list has only one node, return an empty list after deletion. The input list is represented as a sequence of node values, and the output should be the modified list after deletion.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer N, the number of nodes in the list. The second line contains N space-separated integers representing the node values.
statement.outputFormat: The output should be a sequence of node values after deletion, one value per line, preceded by a line containing the count of nodes in the modified list.

constraints.description: Number of nodes in the list

examples.explanation: The head node with value 1 is deleted, and the modified list has 4 nodes with values 2, 3, 4, and 5.
The list has only one node, which is deleted, resulting in an empty list.
The input list is empty, so the output is also an empty list.

hints.text: start by understanding the structure of a doubly linked list, which has both 'next' and 'prev' pointers
consider the case when the list has only one node, and think about how you can modify the 'head' and 'tail' pointers accordingly
if the list has more than one node, you'll need to update the 'next' pointer of the node that comes after the head
you'll also need to update the 'prev' pointer of the node that comes after the head, to point to the new head of the list
the key insight is to realize that you don't need to traverse the entire list to delete the head, you can do it in constant time

editorial.title: Efficient Deletion of Head Node in a Doubly Linked List
editorial.intuition: The problem requires deleting the head of a doubly linked list. The brute force approach involves shifting all elements one position to the left, effectively removing the head. However, this is inefficient and can be improved by simply returning the next node as the new head.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force solution is correct but slow because it requires iterating over the entire list to shift elements, resulting in a time complexity of O(n). This approach is unnecessary and can be optimized.
editorial.betterSolution: A more efficient approach is to recognize that deleting the head node only requires updating the head reference to point to the next node, reducing the time complexity.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves checking for edge cases where the list is empty or contains only one node. If the list has more than one node, it simply returns the number of remaining nodes and prints the elements starting from the second node, effectively deleting the head. This approach has a time complexity of O(n) and a space complexity of O(n) due to the input storage.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by considering the edge cases and the general case. For an empty list or a list with one node, returning 0 is correct. For a list with more than one node, returning the number of remaining nodes and printing the elements starting from the second node correctly deletes the head node.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the optimal solution involves iterating over the input list, checking for edge cases, and then printing the number of remaining nodes and the elements starting from the second node.
commonMistakes: Failing to handle edge cases where the list is empty or contains only one node
Incorrectly shifting elements instead of simply updating the head reference
Not considering the time and space complexity of the solution
Failing to iterate over the input list correctly
